    Ilyushin Il-86

    Ilyushin · Widebody · Retired

    The USSR's first widebody.

    Length59.54 m
    Wingspan48.06 m
    Range5,000 km
    MTOW215 t
    Typical seats350
    Built106
    Our numbers

    Derived metrics

    Computed by aircraft.fyi from the core specification — see how we calculate these.

    Seats per metre#2 of 30
    5.88 seats/m typical seats ÷ fuselage length
    Range per tonne#37 of 40
    23.3 km/t maximum range ÷ MTOW in tonnes
    Span-to-length ratio#36 of 43
    0.81 wingspan ÷ fuselage length
    Weight per metre#22 of 43
    3.61 t/m MTOW ÷ fuselage length
    Range in hours#34 of 40
    5.3 h maximum range ÷ cruise speed
    Weight per seat#20 of 30
    614 kg MTOW ÷ typical seats
    Sense of scale

    How big is it, really?

    The Ilyushin Il-86's wingspan, against things you already know the size of.

    Ilyushin Il-8648.06 m
    Football pitch (width)68 m
    Blue whale25 m
    New Routemaster bus11.2 m
    You (probably)1.75 m
    #20 of 43 by length → #22 by weight → #12 by speed →
    Specification

    The numbers

    Length59.54 m
    Wingspan48.06 m
    Height15.68 m
    MTOW215,000 kg
    Range≈5,000 km
    Typical seatsup to 350
    Engines4 × Kuznetsov NK-86
    First flight22 December 1976
    Built106

    Compare it

    Last verified: 2026-07-14 · Spot an error? business@luck.fyi

    The story

    What makes it different

    The Il-86 was the Soviet Union's first widebody, and it solved a uniquely Soviet problem: airports without infrastructure. Passengers boarded up the aircraft's own built-in airstairs into the lower deck, hung their coats, stowed their own luggage on racks, then climbed internal staircases to the cabin — no jet bridges, no baggage handlers required.

    It carried up to 350 people on trunk routes across the USSR for a quarter century, reliable and universally short on range — its engines were thirsty enough that Moscow to Havana needed multiple stops. Only 106 were built.

    Airline service ended around 2011. A few airframes soldier on with the Russian military as the Il-80 'doomsday' airborne command posts — the Il-86's strangest legacy.

    Variants

    The Ilyushin Il-86 line

    Il-86
    The airliner — retired from passenger service around 2011.
    Il-80
    Airborne command post conversions — a few remain with the Russian military.
    Operators

    Who flies the Ilyushin Il-86

    No current operators — this aircraft's flying days are over. The operators of record are below.

    RURussian Ministry of DefenceIl-80 command-post derivatives.≈4
    RUAeroflotThe principal operator through the Soviet era and the 1990s.—

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
